Chapter 12 narrates the French Reformation as a fresh instance of the book's recurring pattern: truth breaks in on individuals (Lefevre, Farel, Briconnet, Berquin, Calvin) despite institutional Catholic loyalty, faces persecution, and survives through steadfast witnesses even when a leader compromises (Briconnet) or is martyred (Berquin) — culminating in Geneva/Calvin as a durable institutional base for the wider Reformation. The chapter's own argumentative weight rests on the cumulative biographical arc of conversions and martyrdoms, not on any single anecdote; Briconnet's fall functions only as a foil contrasting leader-compromise with flock-fidelity (GC-12-036, already supported), and the closing Louis XVI/Terror passage (paragraphs 55-56) is a moral-providential coda illustrating 'rejected light produces judgment,' a theme pattern in the book but distinct from the central thesis chain (Reformation succession -> Millerite -> sanctuary -> Sabbath) defined in CLAUDE.md. The providential causal claims GC-12-097 and GC-12-116 (France's Reformation-era persecution 'inevitably' producing the Revolution/Terror) are theological/unverifiable and are noted here only for context, not scored as impact items per task scope. Only two claims carry a defense-adjusted anachronistic verdict in this chapter: GC-12-035 (Briconnet's personal peril dramatized) and GC-12-114 (Louis XVI's execution dramatized); both trace to Wylie (History of Protestantism, bk. 13), White's cited 19th-century source, so their severity commentary must register period-typical dramatization rather than invention.

sources.note: 'Marguerite remained within the Catholic Church, pushing for internal reform, while avoiding a full break with the papacy.'

defense.argument: GC's clause is 'The sister of Francis I... accepted the reformed faith,' set in a paragraph that grades degrees of adherence: the king and queen mother only 'appeared for a time to regard it with favor,' while Marguerite 'accepted' it. In 19th-century evangelical idiom 'the reformed faith' denotes the doctrine of justification by grace through faith and the authority of Scripture, not institutional membership in a Reformed/Calvinist church. Under that reading the claim is well supported: Marguerite de Navarre was a committed evangelical inside the Briconnet-Lefevre circle, corresponded with Briconnet on evangelical spirituality, protected reformers throughout her life, and wrote the 'Miroir de l'ame pecheresse,' which the Sorbonne censured as heretical in 1533 (Francis I forced its withdrawal). The verifier's own sources grant her genuine evangelical convictions and contradict only the stronger reading that she formally became a Protestant, a claim White's sentence does not make: Marguerite never left the Catholic Church. Because the assertion is true under the doctrinal sense the words naturally carry, the anachronism charge rests on over-literalizing 'reformed faith' as institutional conversion. The distinction White draws in the same sentence (sister 'accepted' vs. king/mother merely 'regarded with favor') tracks the historical reality that her evangelical commitment was deeper and more durable than her brother's political flirtation.

claims.quote: Now the stake was set up. The bishop of Meaux, forced to choose between the fire and recantation, accepted the easier path;
claims.claim_text: When faced with execution by burning or recantation, the bishop of Meaux (Guillaume Briconnet) chose to recant.

verification.rationale: GC dramatizes Briçonnet as 'forced to choose between the fire and recantation'. Modern scholarship shows he appeared before the Paris Parlement, gave guarantees to the Sorbonne that public Scripture instruction would cease, and was acquitted of heresy in November 1525 (fined 200 livres) — he was never condemned to the stake. The 'stake or recant' framing traces to White's Reformation sources.
verification.modern_consensus: Briçonnet submitted to Parlement pressure and issued anti-Lutheran synodal measures; acquitted 1525, remained bishop until his death in 1534. No death sentence.

sources.note: Acquitted Nov 1525, fined 200 livres; gave guarantees to the Sorbonne

defense.argument: The strongest reading takes 'forced to choose between the fire and recantation, accepted the easier path' as rhetorical antithesis, not a claim that a tribunal literally sentenced Briconnet to death-or-abjuration. At Meaux the stake was in fact 'set up': Jean Leclerc and other members of his flock were burned, so a bishop championing reform did face a real existential choice, press on and share his people's fate or draw back. And he did draw back: under Sorbonne/Parlement pressure he gave guarantees that public Scripture instruction would cease and issued anti-Lutheran synodal measures, a functional recantation of his reforming program, while his flock 'remained steadfast.' That substance, leader falls and followers stand, is accurate. The defense fails only on the specific 'fire or recantation' mechanism: modern scholarship and the verifier's own Baird source show Briconnet was never in personal danger of the stake, was acquitted of heresy in November 1525, fined 200 livres, and remained bishop of Meaux until his death in 1534. Protected by episcopal rank and by royal and Marguerite favor, he did not recant to save himself from burning. The 'held between fire and recantation' peril is a Wylie/D'Aubigne dramatization (the passage traces to Wylie, book 13), so the anachronistic verdict stands. Severity is low, since the underlying event, the bishop's submission under persecution, is real; the exaggeration is confined to his personal danger.

impact.reasoning: GC dramatizes Briconnet as personally facing 'the fire or recantation,' but modern scholarship shows he was acquitted of heresy in 1525, fined, and remained bishop of Meaux until 1534 with no death sentence ever pronounced — the personal mortal peril is invented even though his real capitulation to Sorbonne pressure (ceasing public Scripture instruction) is accurate. The dramatization follows Wylie's account (White's named source, bk. 13), so this reflects period historiographical embellishment rather than fabrication from nothing. Deleting the claim leaves the chapter's contrast intact: GC-12-036 (flock remained steadfast, martyrs died) stands independently and already carries the chapter's rhetorical point about leader-compromise versus lay fidelity, so the argument is not load-bearing on this specific dramatized peril.

claims.claim_text: Olivetan is quoted telling Calvin there are only two kinds of religion: man-invented religions of works, and the Bible's religion of salvation by free grace.

verification.rationale: Olivétan's 'two religions' speech derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source; not independently documented as a verbatim utterance.

claims.quote: I will have none of your new doctrines,” exclaimed Calvin; “think you that I have lived in error all my days?
claims.claim_text: John Calvin is quoted responding to Olivetan by rejecting the 'new doctrines' and asking if Olivetan thought he had lived in error all his days.

verification.rationale: Calvin's rejoinder rejecting 'new doctrines' derives from D'Aubigné's narrative, White's cited source.

claims.quote: Confession, penance, were resorted to in vain; they could not reconcile the soul with God.

claims.quote: Calvin, chancing one day to visit one of the public squares, witnessed there the burning of a heretic.

verification.rationale: The scene of Calvin witnessing a heretic's burning in a Paris square is a D'Aubigné-derived narrative detail, White's cited source; not independently documented.

claims.quote: O Father,” he cried, “His sacrifice has appeased Thy wrath; His blood has washed away my impurities; His cross has borne my curse; His death has atoned for me.
claims.claim_text: John Calvin is quoted praying that Christ's sacrifice appeased God's wrath and atoned for him.

verification.rationale: Calvin's atonement prayer derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources; not independently located in Calvin's works.

verification.rationale: Calvin received the tonsure and the chaplaincy of the altar of La Gésine in Noyon cathedral in May 1521, at age twelve — confirmed by standard biography.

claims.claim_text: John Calvin never received consecration or performed priestly duties, but held clerical title and received an allowance for it.

verification.rationale: Calvin held the benefice/title and drew its income without ordination or priestly duties, the actual duties performed by others — standard account of clerical benefices held by minors.

claims.quote: Now, feeling that he could never become a priest, he turned for a time to the study of law, but finally abandoned this purpose, and determined to devote his life to the gospel.
claims.claim_text: John Calvin turned to studying law for a time before abandoning it to devote his life to the gospel.

claims.quote: A few months, and he was again in Paris.

claims.quote: For two years the word of God was preached in the capital; but while there were many who accepted the gospel, the majority of the people rejected it.

verification.rationale: The 'two years' of preaching in Paris with majority rejection derives from D'Aubigné's narrative, White's cited source; the precise duration is not firmly documented (the intense evangelical preaching centered on 1533).

claims.quote: Francis had made a show of toleration, merely to serve his own purposes, and the papists succeeded in regaining the ascendency. Again the churches were closed, and the stake was set up.

claims.quote: Traveling southward, he again found refuge in the dominions of Margaret.

claims.quote: Instead of advancing the reform, this zealous but ill-judged movement brought ruin, not only upon its propagators, but upon the friends of the reformed faith throughout France. claims.quote: The scaffolds were distributed over all the quarters of Paris, and the burnings followed on successive days, the design being to spread the terror of heresy by spreading the executions.

claims.quote: The cruelties that were inflicted upon the innocent Protestants by the Catholics, accumulated in a weight of retribution, and in after-centuries wrought the very doom they had predicted to be impending, upon the king, his government, and his subjects; but it was brought about by infidels, and by the papists themselves.

verification.rationale: Six Protestants were burned in Paris in conjunction with the 21 January 1535 procession; the staging near the route, timed to the day's ceremonial, is attested.

claims.quote: I only believe in what the prophets and the apostles formerly preached, and what all the company of saints believed. My faith has a confidence in God which will resist all the powers of hell.
claims.claim_text: One of the Protestant martyrs executed during the January 1535 procession is quoted refusing to recant, affirming faith in the teaching of the prophets and apostles.

verification.rationale: The martyr's confession of faith in the prophets and apostles derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

claims.quote: Upon reaching their starting-point at the royal palace, the crowd dispersed, and the king and the prelates withdrew, well satisfied with the day’s proceedings, and congratulating themselves that the work now begun would be continued to the complete destruction of heresy.
claims.claim_text: After the January 1535 procession returned to the royal palace, King Francis I and church prelates withdrew satisfied, intending to continue the persecution until heresy was completely destroyed.

verification.rationale: The closing scene of king and prelates withdrawing satisfied and resolved to continue the repression derives from Wylie's narrative, White's cited source, and matches the documented intensification of persecution.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

claims.quote: On the 21st of January, 1793, two hundred and fifty-eight years from the very day that fully committed France to the persecution of the Reformers, another procession, with a far different purpose, passed through the streets of Paris.
claims.claim_text: Louis XVI was executed in Paris on January 21, 1793, exactly 258 years after the January 21, 1535 procession that committed France to persecuting Protestants.

verification.rationale: Louis XVI was executed on 21 January 1793 in Paris; 1793 minus 1535 = 258 years, and both events fell on 21 January. The date arithmetic and coincidence are accurate.

claims.quote: Louis XVI., struggling hand to hand with his jailers and executioners, was dragged forward to the block, and there held down by main force till the axe had fallen, and his dissevered head rolled on the scaffold.
claims.claim_text: King Louis XVI struggled physically with his jailers and executioners before being forced to the block and beheaded, his severed head rolling on the scaffold.

verification.rationale: GC (following Wylie) depicts Louis XVI 'struggling hand to hand with his jailers and executioners... dragged forward... held down by main force'. The dominant modern account has Louis go to the scaffold with composure, address the crowd (drums drowning him), and submit; his only resistance was a brief objection to having his hands bound, which he accepted on his confessor's counsel. The violent-struggle version reflects 19th-c dramatized historiography.
verification.modern_consensus: Louis XVI met his execution with dignity; he addressed the crowd and submitted, after briefly resisting the binding of his hands.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie's account portrays the king as physically forced to the block.

sources.title: On This Day in 1793, Revolutionaries Executed the King — Smithsonian Magazine

defense.argument: There is a genuine kernel behind Wylie's dramatization, so this is not pure invention: at the scaffold Louis XVI did physically resist. When the executioners moved to bind his hands he recoiled, pushed back the man holding the rope, and cried 'Never! Never!', documented by the executioner Sanson's own family accounts. So 'struggling... with his executioners' amplifies a real moment of resistance. But the defense fails because the two best eyewitnesses both contradict the full picture of a man 'dragged forward to the block' and 'held down by main force till the axe had fallen.' Louis's confessor Edgeworth, present on the scaffold, records that after the brief protest Louis submitted ('So be it, then'), allowed his hands to be bound, mounted the scaffold and tried to address the crowd until the drums drowned him. Executioner Charles-Henri Sanson wrote on 20 February 1793 that the king 'bore all this with a composure and a firmness which has surprised us all,' and Henri Sanson explained the binding was ordered only lest an involuntary movement 'spoil his execution,' not because they feared he would fight. Modern historiography is not divided on this, so the verdict is 'anachronistic' (Wylie reproduced an amplified strand contradicted by the primary eyewitnesses) rather than 'disputed.' Severity is low: the resistance was real but brief, not the hand-to-hand struggle-to-the-block that GC quotes.

new_sources.title: The Executioner's Account of Louis XVI's Execution (Charles-Henri Sanson, letter of 20 Feb 1793)
new_sources.note: Sanson: the king 'bore all this with a composure and a firmness which has surprised us all'; hands bound only lest an involuntary movement spoil the execution.

new_sources.title: The Execution of Louis XVI - Henry Essex Edgeworth (1793 eyewitness account)
new_sources.note: Confessor's eyewitness memoir: after briefly resisting the binding of his hands the king submitted and died with composure.

new_sources.title: Trial and Execution of Louis XVI - World History Encyclopedia

impact.reasoning: GC's 'struggling hand to hand... dragged forward... held down by main force' directly contradicts the two best primary eyewitnesses (the king's confessor Edgeworth and executioner Sanson), both of whom describe Louis XVI submitting with composure after a brief, quickly-resolved objection to having his hands bound — a materially different portrayal of how a specific, well-documented historical figure met his death. Severity is higher than a typical anachronistic slip because this is a comparatively recent, richly attested event (unlike Briconnet's fate) where the contradiction is stark and the dramatization inverts the historical man's demeanor; it still traces to Wylie (bk. 13, White's cited source) rather than invention, so it reflects 19th-century dramatized historiography, not authorial fabrication. Centrality is moderate, not high: the passage functions as the vivid capstone of the chapter's own retribution parallel (1535 persecution procession vs. 1793 Terror procession) but is not part of the book's enumerated central thesis chain (Reformation succession -> Millerite -> sanctuary -> Sabbath); deleting the dramatized detail leaves the parallel's core facts (same date, same city, king executed, Terror followed) fully intact via GC-12-113/115 (both supported), so the chapter's providential argument is not load-bearing on this specific embellishment.

claims.quote: near the same spot two thousand and eight hundred human beings perished by the guillotine during the bloody days of the Reign of Terror.
claims.claim_text: Approximately 2,800 people were executed by guillotine near the same location in Paris during the Reign of Terror.

verification.rationale: About 2,600-2,800 people were guillotined in Paris during the Reign of Terror (modern estimate ~2,625). GC's figure of 2,800 is in the right range; 'near the same spot' loosely fits the Place de la Révolution, though executions also occurred elsewhere in Paris.
verification.modern_consensus: ~2,625 executed by guillotine in Paris during the Terror.

sources.title: The French Revolution executed royals and nobles... — The Conversation

claims.quote: When France rejected the gift of heaven, she sowed the seeds of anarchy and ruin; and the inevitable outworking of cause and effect resulted in the Revolution and the Reign of Terror.
claims.claim_text: France's rejection and suppression of Protestantism in the 16th century is claimed to have causally led, through an inevitable chain of cause and effect, to the French Revolution and the Reign of Terror.

verification.rationale: Providential causal thesis: that France's 16th-c rejection of Protestantism 'inevitably' produced the Revolution and Reign of Terror. This is a theological reading of history, not an empirically testable causal claim; modern historiography does not endorse the inevitability or the direct causal chain.
verification.modern_consensus: Historians attribute the Revolution to Enlightenment ideas plus fiscal, social, and political crises, not to divine retribution for Reformation-era persecution.

claims.quote: Long before the persecution excited by the placards, the bold and ardent Farel had been forced to flee from the land of his birth. He repaired to Switzerland, and by his labors, seconding the work of Zwingle, he helped to turn the scale in favor of the Reformation.
claims.claim_text: William Farel was forced to flee France before the Affair of the Placards, went to Switzerland, and assisted Zwingli's reform work there.

verification.rationale: Farel fled France and worked in French-speaking Switzerland from the mid-1520s, advancing the Reformation alongside Zwingli's Swiss work (Zwingli d. 1531).

claims.quote: With the assistance of other exiles, the writings of the German Reformers were translated into the French language, and together with the French Bible, were printed in large quantities. By colporteurs, these works were sold extensively in France.
claims.claim_text: French Protestant exiles translated German Reformers' writings into French and printed them in large quantities along with French Bibles, which colporteurs sold extensively within France.

verification.rationale: French exiles (notably at Neuchâtel) translated and printed Reformation works and French Bibles, distributed into France by colporteurs — well-documented Reformation book trade.

claims.quote: Farel entered upon his work in Switzerland in the humble guise of a schoolmaster.
claims.claim_text: William Farel began his reform work in Switzerland disguised as a humble schoolmaster.

verification.rationale: Farel began his Swiss work under the humble guise of a schoolmaster (e.g. at Aigle, using a pseudonym).

claims.quote: That cannot be the gospel of Christ,” urged the priests, “seeing the preaching of it does not bring peace, but war.
claims.claim_text: Priests opposing Farel's preaching in Switzerland reportedly argued that his message could not be the gospel of Christ because it brought war rather than peace.

verification.rationale: The priests' argument that a gospel bringing war cannot be Christ's derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

claims.quote: More than once he was set upon by the rabble, and beaten almost to death.
claims.claim_text: William Farel was attacked by mobs on more than one occasion in Switzerland and beaten nearly to death.

verification.rationale: Farel was repeatedly assaulted by hostile crowds and beaten during his Swiss preaching campaigns.

claims.quote: The cities of Morat and Neuchatel also renounced the Romish rites, and removed the idolatrous images from their churches.
claims.claim_text: The Swiss cities of Morat and Neuchatel renounced Catholic rites and removed religious images from their churches as a result of Farel's work.

verification.rationale: Farel's preaching led to the Reformation and removal of images in Neuchâtel (iconoclasm of 23 October 1530); Morat (Murten) likewise adopted the Reformation around this period.

sources.title: 23 octobre 1530 - Neuchâtel se rallie à la Réforme — Herodote.net

claims.quote: Farel had long desired to plant the Protestant standard in Geneva.
claims.claim_text: William Farel had long desired to establish Protestantism in Geneva.

verification.rationale: Farel long sought to bring the Reformation to Geneva, which he entered in the early 1530s.

claims.quote: Then with a single companion he entered Geneva. But only two sermons was he permitted to preach.
claims.claim_text: William Farel entered Geneva with a single companion and was permitted to preach only two sermons before opposition arose.

verification.rationale: Farel first entered Geneva (1532) with a companion and met immediate clerical opposition after brief preaching.

claims.quote: The priests, having vainly endeavored to secure his condemnation by the civil authorities, summoned him before an ecclesiastical council, to which they came with arms concealed under their robes, determined to take his life.
claims.claim_text: Priests in Geneva, unable to get Farel condemned by civil authorities, summoned him before an ecclesiastical council and came armed under their robes intending to kill him.

verification.rationale: Farel was summoned before a Geneva ecclesiastical council in 1532 amid clerical hostility that nearly cost his life; the detail of concealed weapons is D'Aubigné/Wylie dramatization of a genuinely dangerous confrontation.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

claims.quote: Outside the hall, a furious mob, with clubs and swords, was gathered to make sure of his death if he should succeed in escaping the council.
claims.claim_text: Outside the ecclesiastical council in Geneva, an armed mob gathered to kill Farel should he escape the council.

verification.rationale: The armed mob outside the council is a Wylie/D'Aubigné detail, White's cited source, dramatizing the real threat to Farel in Geneva 1532.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

claims.quote: Early next morning he was conducted, with his companion, across the lake to a place of safety.
claims.claim_text: William Farel and his companion were escorted across Lake Geneva to safety the morning after the council confrontation.

verification.rationale: Farel and his companion were escorted to safety and left Geneva after the 1532 confrontation.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

claims.quote: For the next trial a lowlier instrument was chosen,—a young man, so humble in appearance that he was coldly treated even by the professed friends of reform.
claims.claim_text: After Farel's failed first attempt, a young man of humble appearance (Antoine Froment) was chosen for a new attempt to evangelize Geneva, and was treated coldly even by professed reform sympathizers.

verification.rationale: Antoine Froment, a young evangelist of humble bearing, was sent to Geneva after Farel's first attempt.

claims.quote: Froment began his work as a schoolmaster.
claims.claim_text: Antoine Froment began his evangelistic work in Geneva as a schoolmaster.

verification.rationale: Froment went to Geneva (Nov 1532) and opened a school, using it as a platform for evangelical preaching.

claims.quote: the preachers returned, and through their labors the Protestant worship was finally established in Geneva.
claims.claim_text: Following Froment's schoolmaster work, Protestant preachers returned to Geneva and Protestant worship was finally established there.

verification.rationale: After Froment's groundwork, the Protestant preachers returned (backed by Bern) and Protestant worship was ultimately established in Geneva (official 1535/36).

claims.quote: The city had already declared for the Reformation, when Calvin, after various wanderings and vicissitudes, entered its gates.
claims.claim_text: Geneva had already officially declared for the Reformation by the time John Calvin arrived there.

verification.rationale: Geneva officially adopted the Reformation (May 1536) before Calvin arrived there (July/August 1536).

claims.quote: Returning from a last visit to his birthplace, he was on his way to Basel, when, finding the direct road occupied by the armies of Charles V., he was forced to take the circuitous route by Geneva.
claims.claim_text: John Calvin's direct route to Basel was blocked by the armies of Charles V, forcing him to travel via Geneva instead.

verification.rationale: In 1536 war between Francis I and Charles V blocked Calvin's direct route, forcing him to detour through Geneva on his way to Basel/Strasbourg.

sources.title: John Calvin: Arrival in Geneva — Christian Study Library

claims.quote: While the people of Geneva had cast off the authority of Rome, they were not so ready to renounce the vices that had flourished under her rule.
claims.claim_text: Although Geneva had formally rejected papal authority, its people had not abandoned the vices that had flourished under Catholic rule.

verification.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Largely interpretive/moralizing, but it embeds a checkable fact: Geneva had cast off papal authority (true, 1535/36). The claim that the populace retained old vices matches Calvin's own documented complaints, though the moral judgment is not independently quantifiable. Mild extractor blind spot noted; embedded fact is supported.

claims.quote: that the hand of God was stretched down from heaven, that it lay hold of him, and fixed him irrevocably to the place he was so impatient to leave.
claims.claim_text: Calvin is quoted describing Farel's admonition to stay in Geneva as feeling like the hand of God reaching down and fixing him irrevocably to the place.

verification.rationale: Calvin's own testimony that he felt 'as if God had from heaven laid his mighty hand upon me to arrest me' when Farel adjured him to stay in Geneva is well attested.

sources.title: John Calvin: Arrival in Geneva — Christian Study Library

claims.quote: The anathemas of the pope thundered against Geneva, and mighty nations threatened it with destruction.
claims.claim_text: The pope pronounced anathemas against Geneva, and powerful nations threatened the city with destruction.

verification.rationale: Reformation Geneva faced papal condemnation and external military threat (notably from the Duke of Savoy) in this period.

claims.quote: At this time, the order of the Jesuits was created, the most cruel, unscrupulous, and powerful of all the champions of popery.
claims.claim_text: The Society of Jesus (Jesuits) was founded around this time (1540) as a Catholic religious order to counter Protestantism.

verification.rationale: The Society of Jesus was founded and papally approved in 1540 (bull Regimini militantis Ecclesiae, 27 Sept 1540). GC's date is correct; 'most cruel, unscrupulous' is polemical characterization, not a factual claim.

claims.quote: They established colleges for the sons of princes and nobles, and schools for the common people; and the children of Protestant parents were drawn into an observance of popish rites.
claims.claim_text: The Jesuits established colleges for the nobility and schools for common people, drawing children of Protestant parents into Catholic religious observance.

verification.rationale: The Jesuits became a major Counter-Reformation educational force, running colleges for elites and schools for common people, drawing pupils (including from Protestant families) into Catholic practice.

claims.quote: Under various disguises the Jesuits worked their way into offices of state, climbing up to be the counselors of kings, and shaping the policy of nations.
claims.claim_text: Jesuits worked their way into government offices, becoming counselors to kings and shaping national policy.

verification.rationale: Jesuits did gain influence at Catholic courts (royal confessors, advisors). GC's 'under various disguises... shaping the policy of nations' overlays anti-Jesuit polemic on a factual core.

claims.quote: To give them greater power, a bull was issued re-establishing the Inquisition.
claims.claim_text: A papal bull re-established the Inquisition (the Roman Inquisition, 1542) to give the Jesuits and church authorities greater power against Protestantism.

verification.rationale: Pope Paul III's bull Licet ab initio (21 July 1542) established the Roman Inquisition (Congregation of the Holy Office). GC's linkage of this specifically to empowering the Jesuits is White's framing; the bull was Cardinal Carafa's initiative, not a Jesuit instrument.
verification.modern_consensus: Roman Inquisition established 1542 by Licet ab initio; distinct from the Jesuit order.

claims.quote: In many countries, thousands upon thousands of the very flower of the nation, the purest and noblest, the most intellectual and highly educated, pious and devoted pastors, industrious and patriotic citizens, brilliant scholars, talented artists, skilful artisans, were slain, or forced to flee to other lands.
claims.claim_text: The re-established Inquisition resulted in thousands of accomplished and educated people across many countries being killed or forced into exile.

verification.rationale: Counter-Reformation persecution killed or exiled large numbers of Protestants across multiple countries, including many educated and skilled people.

claims.quote: it was Holland on her sand-banks by the northern sea, wrestling against the tyranny of Spain, then the greatest and most opulent of kingdoms;
claims.claim_text: The Netherlands (Holland) fought against Spanish rule, Spain then being the greatest and richest of kingdoms, and contributed to securing the Reformation.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Dutch Revolt against Spanish rule (Spain then Europe's greatest power) is textbook fact and advanced the Protestant cause in the Netherlands.

claims.quote: it was bleak, sterile Sweden, that gained victories for the Reformation.
claims.claim_text: Sweden played a significant role in achieving victories for the Protestant Reformation.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Sweden adopted Lutheranism under the Vasa monarchy and later, under Gustavus Adolphus, decisively aided the Protestant cause in the Thirty Years' War.

claims.quote: For nearly thirty years, Calvin labored at Geneva; first to establish there a church adhering to the morality of the Bible, and then for the advancement of the Reformation throughout Europe.
claims.claim_text: John Calvin worked in Geneva for nearly thirty years (approximately 1536-1564), first establishing a church there and then working for the Reformation's advancement across Europe.

verification.rationale: Calvin worked at Geneva from 1536 (with a 1538-1541 exile) until his death in 1564 — spanning nearly thirty years, first building the Genevan church, then promoting the Reformation across Europe.

claims.quote: His course as a public leader was not faultless, nor were his doctrines free from error.
claims.claim_text: Calvin's leadership and doctrines are characterized as imperfect and containing errors.

verification.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Evaluative/interpretive judgment that Calvin's leadership was not faultless and his doctrines not error-free. This is a value judgment, not an empirically testable proposition; extractor's non-flag is appropriate.

claims.quote: The city of Calvin became a refuge for the hunted Reformers of all Western Europe.
claims.claim_text: Geneva, under Calvin, became a refuge for persecuted Protestant Reformers from across Western Europe.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Calvin's Geneva became a refuge and training center for persecuted Protestants from across Western Europe.

claims.quote: John Knox, the brave Scotch Reformer, not a few of the English Puritans, the Protestants of Holland and of Spain, and the Huguenots of France, carried from Geneva the torch of truth to lighten the darkness of their native lands.
claims.claim_text: John Knox, along with a number of English Puritans, Dutch and Spanish Protestants, and French Huguenots, took refuge in Geneva and carried Reformation influence back to their home countries.

verification.rationale: John Knox and Marian-exile English Protestants, together with Dutch, Spanish, and French Huguenot refugees, sojourned in Geneva and carried Reformed influence home. 'Puritans' is a mildly anachronistic label for the mid-century English exiles.
